Authors: Xinglang Yin; Xiao Xu; Xiaoyu Guo; Chunsen Li; Xiaofei Tong; Bo Xiang; Zizhao Yuan; Yanqi Lin; Hongcheng Shi

Journal: Tectonics

DOI: 10.1029/2025tc009090

Relevance: 0.559 (consider)

Abstract The mechanisms of rapid exhumation of North Himalayan gneiss domes remain debated. We integrate phase equilibrium modeling, teleseismic tomography, and geodynamic simulation to reconstruct episodic exhumation paths and elucidate the underlying mechanisms. Our results confirm the previously inferred P‐T‐t evolution for the mid‐upper crust Greater Himalayan Sequence (GHS), featuring rapid near‐isothermal decompression during retrograde metamorphism followed by near‐isobaric cooling. Geodynamic modeling demonstrates that sustained intra‐crustal high temperatures trigger pervasive partial melting and pronounced rheological weakening within the middle‐lower level of upper crust. Consequently, extensive crustal melting and associated weakening are prerequisites for enabling exhumation. Sustained convergence and resistance from the Lhasa terrane induce prolonged shortening and thickening of the weakened crust. This culminates in rapid exhumation, driving upper‐crustal extension. Modeling reveals two primary exhumation pathways: (a) direct emplacement near the suture zone, forming the Yardoi dome, and (b) lateral migration facilitated by upper‐crustal shear zones along low‐viscosity channels, transporting material to the GHS and beyond. We conclude that crustal weakening induced by partial melting, in conjunction with the ongoing convergence of the Indian Plate, constitutes the primary control on multi‐stage exhumation events in the Himalayan orogen. The occurrence of multiple exhumation episodes requires successive thermal perturbations to regenerate these low‐viscosity zones.

item.title: Comparing Slab Viscous Dissipation Under Slab Distributed Weakening and Slab Segmentation Scenarios
item.dc:creator: Wenfa Zhou
Jiashun Hu
Keqing Li
Ting Yang
item.description: Abstract Subduction of cold, dense oceanic lithosphere is a key driver of plate tectonics and mantle convection. However, how much gravitational potential energy is internally dissipated by slab deformation during subduction remains debated and largely depends on the mechanisms of slab weakening. We develop two‐dimensional geodynamic models to systematically investigate viscous dissipation within subducting slabs under two end‐member scenarios: slab distributed weakening controlled by plastic yielding, and slab segmentation controlled by outer‐rise faulting and grain size damage. In distributed weakening models, ∼20% of the gravitational potential energy is consumed by slab viscous dissipation, which is independent of the viscosity of the slab, consistent with previous laboratory and numerical studies. In segmentation models caused by grain size evolution and serpentinization, slab viscous dissipation increases significantly, with the dissipation ratio reaching up to 40% . Slab segmentation alters the relationship between viscous dissipation and both effective viscosity and subduction velocity, breaking the conventional scaling laws based on fixed slab geometry. Moreover, by calculating viscous dissipation in global subduction zones, we infer that the average viscosity of subducting slabs does not exceed if slab segmentation is the dominant mode for global subduction zones. We estimate that 60%–80% of the gravitational potential energy is transferred to the surrounding mantle to drive mantle convection, regardless of the slab mode. These findings emphasize that accounting for more realistic rheological structures, including plastic yielding and slab segmentation in subduction models, is crucial for accurately quantifying energy partitioning within the Earth and understanding plate–mantle interactions. Published: 2026-03-17T10:34:47+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.554 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wenfa Zhou; Jiashun Hu; Keqing Li; Ting Yang

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2025gc012783

Relevance: 0.554 (consider)

Abstract Subduction of cold, dense oceanic lithosphere is a key driver of plate tectonics and mantle convection. However, how much gravitational potential energy is internally dissipated by slab deformation during subduction remains debated and largely depends on the mechanisms of slab weakening. We develop two‐dimensional geodynamic models to systematically investigate viscous dissipation within subducting slabs under two end‐member scenarios: slab distributed weakening controlled by plastic yielding, and slab segmentation controlled by outer‐rise faulting and grain size damage. In distributed weakening models, ∼20% of the gravitational potential energy is consumed by slab viscous dissipation, which is independent of the viscosity of the slab, consistent with previous laboratory and numerical studies. In segmentation models caused by grain size evolution and serpentinization, slab viscous dissipation increases significantly, with the dissipation ratio reaching up to 40% . Slab segmentation alters the relationship between viscous dissipation and both effective viscosity and subduction velocity, breaking the conventional scaling laws based on fixed slab geometry. Moreover, by calculating viscous dissipation in global subduction zones, we infer that the average viscosity of subducting slabs does not exceed if slab segmentation is the dominant mode for global subduction zones. We estimate that 60%–80% of the gravitational potential energy is transferred to the surrounding mantle to drive mantle convection, regardless of the slab mode. These findings emphasize that accounting for more realistic rheological structures, including plastic yielding and slab segmentation in subduction models, is crucial for accurately quantifying energy partitioning within the Earth and understanding plate–mantle interactions.

item.title: Calcium isotopes and thermodynamic calculations decipher fluorite mineralization in hydrothermal systems
item.dc:creator: Bin Xiao
Hao Zou
Di Yang
Ye Wan
Jianghan Wu
Jinxiang Shen
Franco Pirajno
Huidong Yu
Nuru Said
Huawen Cao
Fang Liu
Zhiwu Li
Zhaofeng Zhang
item.description: Fluorite mineralization, an economically significant process, remains insufficiently understood because traditional geochemical tracers are limited in resolving Ca sources and depositional mechanisms within complex hydrothermal systems. In this study, we integrate calcium (Ca) isotopes with strontium–neodymium (Sr–Nd) isotopes, thermodynamic calculations, and in-situ optical observations to investigate the mineralization and precipitation mechanisms of diverse fluorite deposits, hosted in magmatic, volcanic, and carbonate rocks. Our results reveal considerable variations in δ 44/40 Ca values (0.90–1.89‰) among the deposits. These variations are governed primarily by fluid sources: elevated δ 44/40 Ca values (up to 1.89‰) reflect derivation from ancient evaporitic brines, whereas lower values indicate contributions from crustal or mantle sources. Additionally, equilibrium isotope fractionation during fluorite precipitation (0.21–0.44‰) further influences the δ 44/40 Ca values. Importantly, Ca isotopes demonstrate that Ca is derived predominantly from mineralization fluids rather than from host rocks, even in systems exhibiting strong hydrothermal alteration. To validate the model proposing both F and Ca derived from fluids, this study conducted thermodynamic calculations and in-situ optical observations. The results demonstrate that fluorite solubility exhibits temperature-dependence. Furthermore, specific ions in geological fluids (particularly Al 3+ and H + ) could significantly enhance fluorite dissolution through interactions with F − (via complexation and protonation). These findings indicate that Ca isotopes can serve as effective tracers for Ca sources in hydrothermal mineralization systems, and the coexistence mechanism of Ca and F can fully account for large-scale, highly efficient fluorite mineralization. This study provides the first evidence from Ca isotopes and thermodynamics for the cooling and decompression mineralization model. Published: 2026-03-17T06:18:31+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.552 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Bin Xiao; Hao Zou; Di Yang; Ye Wan; Jianghan Wu; Jinxiang Shen; Franco Pirajno; Huidong Yu; Nuru Said; Huawen Cao; Fang Liu; Zhiwu Li; Zhaofeng Zhang

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.03.031

Relevance: 0.552 (consider)

Fluorite mineralization, an economically significant process, remains insufficiently understood because traditional geochemical tracers are limited in resolving Ca sources and depositional mechanisms within complex hydrothermal systems. In this study, we integrate calcium (Ca) isotopes with strontium–neodymium (Sr–Nd) isotopes, thermodynamic calculations, and in-situ optical observations to investigate the mineralization and precipitation mechanisms of diverse fluorite deposits, hosted in magmatic, volcanic, and carbonate rocks. Our results reveal considerable variations in δ 44/40 Ca values (0.90–1.89‰) among the deposits. These variations are governed primarily by fluid sources: elevated δ 44/40 Ca values (up to 1.89‰) reflect derivation from ancient evaporitic brines, whereas lower values indicate contributions from crustal or mantle sources. Additionally, equilibrium isotope fractionation during fluorite precipitation (0.21–0.44‰) further influences the δ 44/40 Ca values. Importantly, Ca isotopes demonstrate that Ca is derived predominantly from mineralization fluids rather than from host rocks, even in systems exhibiting strong hydrothermal alteration. To validate the model proposing both F and Ca derived from fluids, this study conducted thermodynamic calculations and in-situ optical observations. The results demonstrate that fluorite solubility exhibits temperature-dependence. Furthermore, specific ions in geological fluids (particularly Al 3+ and H + ) could significantly enhance fluorite dissolution through interactions with F − (via complexation and protonation). These findings indicate that Ca isotopes can serve as effective tracers for Ca sources in hydrothermal mineralization systems, and the coexistence mechanism of Ca and F can fully account for large-scale, highly efficient fluorite mineralization. This study provides the first evidence from Ca isotopes and thermodynamics for the cooling and decompression mineralization model.

item.title: First-order control of basaltic sources on the diversity of Archean TTG magmatism
item.dc:creator: Wei Wang
Peter A. Cawood
Shuwen Liu
Denggang Lu
Xueyin Liang
Weibo Yang
Jiachen Yao
Lei Gao
Guozheng Sun
item.description: Archean tonalite-trondhjemite-granodiorite (TTG) gneisses hold the key to unravel the origin of early continental crust. While partial melting of a single basaltic source at different pressures is usually invoked for different TTG gneisses, the effects of source heterogeneity and the competing roles of melting pressure and magma differentiation remain unconstrained. Here, three groups of late Neoarchean tonalitic to trondhjemitic gneisses, showing diverse compositions covering almost the whole chemical spectrum of global TTG gneisses, were identified in the Jiaobei terrane, North China Craton. Field geological, geochemical, and thermodynamic and trace element modeling reveal that they were generated by the partial melting of different basaltic sources at diverse melting pressures, but subjected to limited effects of magma differentiation. More importantly, both the modeling results and the global synchronicity of compositional changes of TTG gneisses and tholeiitic rocks indicate that (1) the compositional variation of partial melts from any single basaltic source is too limited to cover the overall spectrum of Archean tonalitic to trondhjemitic gneisses; and (2) while melting pressure and magma differentiation do contribute, it is the basaltic sources that exert a first-order control on the overall diversity of Archean TTG magmatism. In particular, our newly defined low-Ti-HREE TTG gneisses can only be produced by high pressure melting of low-Ti-HREE basaltic sources. Their sporadic appearance at ca. 3.8–3.7 Ga and ca. 3.5 Ga, but mostly after ca. 3.2 Ga may mark diachronous development of Archean thickened continental crust globally. Published: 2026-03-15T15:44:23+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.552 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wei Wang; Peter A. Cawood; Shuwen Liu; Denggang Lu; Xueyin Liang; Weibo Yang; Jiachen Yao; Lei Gao; Guozheng Sun

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123376

Relevance: 0.552 (consider)

Archean tonalite-trondhjemite-granodiorite (TTG) gneisses hold the key to unravel the origin of early continental crust. While partial melting of a single basaltic source at different pressures is usually invoked for different TTG gneisses, the effects of source heterogeneity and the competing roles of melting pressure and magma differentiation remain unconstrained. Here, three groups of late Neoarchean tonalitic to trondhjemitic gneisses, showing diverse compositions covering almost the whole chemical spectrum of global TTG gneisses, were identified in the Jiaobei terrane, North China Craton. Field geological, geochemical, and thermodynamic and trace element modeling reveal that they were generated by the partial melting of different basaltic sources at diverse melting pressures, but subjected to limited effects of magma differentiation. More importantly, both the modeling results and the global synchronicity of compositional changes of TTG gneisses and tholeiitic rocks indicate that (1) the compositional variation of partial melts from any single basaltic source is too limited to cover the overall spectrum of Archean tonalitic to trondhjemitic gneisses; and (2) while melting pressure and magma differentiation do contribute, it is the basaltic sources that exert a first-order control on the overall diversity of Archean TTG magmatism. In particular, our newly defined low-Ti-HREE TTG gneisses can only be produced by high pressure melting of low-Ti-HREE basaltic sources. Their sporadic appearance at ca. 3.8–3.7 Ga and ca. 3.5 Ga, but mostly after ca. 3.2 Ga may mark diachronous development of Archean thickened continental crust globally.

item.title: Tropical mafic rock weathering, riverine lithium isotopes, and the evolution of Cenozoic seawater chemistry
item.dc:creator: Chris T.L. Cheung
Kohen W. Bauer
Brian Beaty
Juan Miguel Guotana
Decibel V. Faustino-Eslava
Boriana Kalderon-Asael
Dan Asael
Karen Luk
Giancarlo DeFrancesco
Sean M. Newby
Yonghui Qin
Noah J. Planavsky
N. Ryan McKenzie
item.description: The terrestrial dissolved riverine lithium isotope (δ 7 Li diss ) flux can provide valuable information on silicate weathering processes, which are directly tied to the long-term carbon cycle, at various spatiotemporal scales. However, a limited understanding of how bedrock lithological composition impacts δ 7 Li diss values hinders the utility of the Li proxy for deep-time weathering reconstructions. Accordingly, we investigated tropical rivers in the Philippines and Mariana Islands that drain either distinctly ultramafic-to-mafic or silicic bedrock. We find that mafic lithologies produce markedly heavier δ 7 Li diss than those draining silicic lithologies, potentially due to preferential formation of Mg-rich clays during incongruent weathering. Furthermore, we observe that known tectonically driven changes in Earth’s global ophiolitic-collisional suture length correlate with the marine δ 7 Li record over the past ∼60 million years. This observation, supported by mass-balance modelling, indicates that variations in mafic bedrock weathering could have been an important influence on Cenozoic seawater chemistry. Published: 2026-03-19T14:12:32+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.552 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chris T.L. Cheung; Kohen W. Bauer; Brian Beaty; Juan Miguel Guotana; Decibel V. Faustino-Eslava; Boriana Kalderon-Asael; Dan Asael; Karen Luk; Giancarlo DeFrancesco; Sean M. Newby; Yonghui Qin; Noah J. Planavsky; N. Ryan McKenzie

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.119917

Relevance: 0.552 (consider)

The terrestrial dissolved riverine lithium isotope (δ 7 Li diss ) flux can provide valuable information on silicate weathering processes, which are directly tied to the long-term carbon cycle, at various spatiotemporal scales. However, a limited understanding of how bedrock lithological composition impacts δ 7 Li diss values hinders the utility of the Li proxy for deep-time weathering reconstructions. Accordingly, we investigated tropical rivers in the Philippines and Mariana Islands that drain either distinctly ultramafic-to-mafic or silicic bedrock. We find that mafic lithologies produce markedly heavier δ 7 Li diss than those draining silicic lithologies, potentially due to preferential formation of Mg-rich clays during incongruent weathering. Furthermore, we observe that known tectonically driven changes in Earth’s global ophiolitic-collisional suture length correlate with the marine δ 7 Li record over the past ∼60 million years. This observation, supported by mass-balance modelling, indicates that variations in mafic bedrock weathering could have been an important influence on Cenozoic seawater chemistry.

item.title: Neodymium stable isotope fractionation in minerals: Implications for Earth’s differentiation, and planetary formation
item.dc:creator: Mark Nestmeyer
Alex J. McCoy-West
item.description: The application of rare Earth element stable isotope compositions has become of increasing interest in geochemistry. Recently, studies have begun exploring variations in the stable 146 Nd /144 Nd isotope ratio in geological samples, with limited isotope fractionation observed in igneous rocks but significantly larger fractionations seen in low temperature systems. Experimental and theoretical studies on the equilibrium isotope fractionation of Nd are widely missing which can support understanding the fractionation of Nd isotopes among Earth’s major reservoirs. Here, we have modelled the isotope fractionation factors for 15 common rock forming and accessory minerals to help understand equilibrium stable isotope fractionation during medium to high temperature processes. Published: 2026-03-16T16:59:08+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.547 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mark Nestmeyer; Alex J. McCoy-West

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.03.022

Relevance: 0.547 (consider)

The application of rare Earth element stable isotope compositions has become of increasing interest in geochemistry. Recently, studies have begun exploring variations in the stable 146 Nd /144 Nd isotope ratio in geological samples, with limited isotope fractionation observed in igneous rocks but significantly larger fractionations seen in low temperature systems. Experimental and theoretical studies on the equilibrium isotope fractionation of Nd are widely missing which can support understanding the fractionation of Nd isotopes among Earth’s major reservoirs. Here, we have modelled the isotope fractionation factors for 15 common rock forming and accessory minerals to help understand equilibrium stable isotope fractionation during medium to high temperature processes.

item.title: Electrical conductivity of MgCO3 under thermodynamic conditions of Earth’s interior: a first-principles investigation
item.dc:creator: Samuel S. M. Santos
Lucy V. C. Assali
João F. Justo
item.description: Abstract The electrical conductivity of magnesite (MgCO3), magnesium carbonate, was investigated under the thermodynamic conditions of the Earth’s interior, within the framework of density functional theory (DFT) and Boltzmann transport theory. The results show that the conductivity of MgCO3 decreases under pressure and increases under the effect of temperature (depth of the lower mantle). Pressure plays a central role in controlling the conductivity of this mineral by modifying its electronic properties, such as the band structure and electronic density of states. Furthermore, the structural phase transition from the low-pressure magnesite-I (M-I) to the high-pressure magnesite-II (M-II) phase leads to a pronounced reduction in the electrical conductivity of MgCO3. The conductivity of the M-I phase is consistently greater than that of the M-II, and this trend persists by modifications in the respective electronic properties as a result of pressure changes. These findings suggest that magnesite (MgCO3) may have a minor contribution to the observed increase in electrical conductivity with depth in the lower mantle. Therefore, the electrical conductivity profile of the lower mantle is likely controlled by other minerals. Published: 2026-03-18T16:09:09+00:00 Venue: American Mineralogist Score: 0.544 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Samuel S. M. Santos; Lucy V. C. Assali; João F. Justo

Journal: American Mineralogist

DOI: 10.2138/am-2024-9719

Relevance: 0.544 (consider)

Abstract The electrical conductivity of magnesite (MgCO3), magnesium carbonate, was investigated under the thermodynamic conditions of the Earth’s interior, within the framework of density functional theory (DFT) and Boltzmann transport theory. The results show that the conductivity of MgCO3 decreases under pressure and increases under the effect of temperature (depth of the lower mantle). Pressure plays a central role in controlling the conductivity of this mineral by modifying its electronic properties, such as the band structure and electronic density of states. Furthermore, the structural phase transition from the low-pressure magnesite-I (M-I) to the high-pressure magnesite-II (M-II) phase leads to a pronounced reduction in the electrical conductivity of MgCO3. The conductivity of the M-I phase is consistently greater than that of the M-II, and this trend persists by modifications in the respective electronic properties as a result of pressure changes. These findings suggest that magnesite (MgCO3) may have a minor contribution to the observed increase in electrical conductivity with depth in the lower mantle. Therefore, the electrical conductivity profile of the lower mantle is likely controlled by other minerals.

item.title: The tectonic affinity of the Shalazhashan Tectonic Zone in northern Alxa, NW China: Implication for the boundary of the southern CAOB
item.dc:creator: Zhenyi Wang
ChunJiao Wu
Pengfei Wang
Mudong Qiao
Yingfu Chen
Jing Li
Yukun Tian
Haisheng Ding
Gang Fang
Tingting Cui
Xianzhen Meng
item.description: The southern boundary of middle segment of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt (CAOB) has been controversial over the past decade, specifically whether it lies along the Enger Us or the Quagan Qulu ophiolitic mélange belt. Previous arguments, based on discrepancies of zircon Hf isotopic compositions from Paleozoic intrusions and geophysical anomalies, have not fully addressed the nature of the basement. To address this issue, field surveys and zircon U-Pb-Hf analyses were conducted to characterize the Precambrian basement of the Shalazhashan Tectonic Zone (STZ) between the two debatable boundaries. Field observations and ages show the Precambrian rocks in the western STZ are not as extensively exposed as previously mapped. A monzogranite yields an age population of 1459 Ma with ε Hf ( t ) values of 2.6 ∼ 6.8, and a quartzite yields a youngest age of 1192 Ma with major peaks at 1.76 Ga, 1.47–1.44 Ga, and 1.29 Ga. The Hf isotopic compositions of Late Paleoproterozoic detrital zircons are different from Alxa Block. Integrating with previous data, the Precambrian basement of STZ shows close tectonic affinity with the basement in southern CAOB to the north and relatively weak affinity with the Alxa Block to the south based on the rock assemblages, detrital zircon age spectra, Lu-Hf isotopic composition of Precambrian zircons, tectonothermal events, as well as the Hf and Nd model ages of the Paleozoic intrusions. This suggests that the Quagan Qulu ophiolitic mélange belt likely represents the significant boundary of different Precambrian geological domains, separating juvenile CAOB from ancient Alxa Block. Published: 2026-03-16T17:11:03+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.543 (consider)

Authors: Zhenyi Wang; ChunJiao Wu; Pengfei Wang; Mudong Qiao; Yingfu Chen; Jing Li; Yukun Tian; Haisheng Ding; Gang Fang; Tingting Cui; Xianzhen Meng

Journal: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.jseaes.2026.107062

Relevance: 0.543 (consider)

The southern boundary of middle segment of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt (CAOB) has been controversial over the past decade, specifically whether it lies along the Enger Us or the Quagan Qulu ophiolitic mélange belt. Previous arguments, based on discrepancies of zircon Hf isotopic compositions from Paleozoic intrusions and geophysical anomalies, have not fully addressed the nature of the basement. To address this issue, field surveys and zircon U-Pb-Hf analyses were conducted to characterize the Precambrian basement of the Shalazhashan Tectonic Zone (STZ) between the two debatable boundaries. Field observations and ages show the Precambrian rocks in the western STZ are not as extensively exposed as previously mapped. A monzogranite yields an age population of 1459 Ma with ε Hf ( t ) values of 2.6 ∼ 6.8, and a quartzite yields a youngest age of 1192 Ma with major peaks at 1.76 Ga, 1.47–1.44 Ga, and 1.29 Ga. The Hf isotopic compositions of Late Paleoproterozoic detrital zircons are different from Alxa Block. Integrating with previous data, the Precambrian basement of STZ shows close tectonic affinity with the basement in southern CAOB to the north and relatively weak affinity with the Alxa Block to the south based on the rock assemblages, detrital zircon age spectra, Lu-Hf isotopic composition of Precambrian zircons, tectonothermal events, as well as the Hf and Nd model ages of the Paleozoic intrusions. This suggests that the Quagan Qulu ophiolitic mélange belt likely represents the significant boundary of different Precambrian geological domains, separating juvenile CAOB from ancient Alxa Block.

item.title: Geochemical characteristics of lithium in geothermal waters and its impact on riverine Li in the southeastern Tibetan Plateau
item.dc:creator: Longfei Xu
Yishan Cheng
Jinchuan Zhang
Yang Liu
Xingxu Zhao
Yuhang Sun
item.description: Variations in seawater lithium isotopes (δ 7 Li) are commonly linked to changes in continental silicate weathering fluxes. However, the influence of geothermal fluid input on riverine lithium, particularly in tectonically active regions, remains poorly constrained. Here, samples from hot springs along the Lancang River (LCR) in the southeastern Tibetan Plateau (TP) were collected for analysis of hydrochemistry, Li isotope composition (δ 7 Li) and water-rock interactions to study the geochemical characteristics of Li in geothermal waters and corresponding impact on riverine Li. Geothermal waters exhibit high Li concentrations (Li geo , 0.04–2.1 mg/L) and low Li isotope ratios (δ 7 Li geo , −0.56 to +12.3‰). High reservoir temperatures along the LCR (93–176 °C) facilitate water-rock interaction and leach more Li from surrounding rocks into geothermal waters. The Li geo and δ 7 Li geo are influenced by fluid residence time and the dissolution-precipitation balance. Geothermal waters with longer residence time have higher Li geo concentrations, whereas secondary mineral formation preferentially sequesters light Li, resulting in the enrichment of isotopically heavy Li in the residual geothermal fluid. The LCR exhibits elevated mean Li concentration (Li diss ) and depressed mean Li isotope ratio (δ 7 Li diss ) relative to global river averages, with both parameters tracking downstream variations in geothermal inputs. The mixing model estimates that the contribution of geothermal waters decreases downstream. The concomitant downstream increase in δ 7 Li diss may reflect either declining geothermal contribution or increased secondary mineral formation. Our study provides evidence for the significant impact of geothermal water on riverine Li, and therefore potentially linked to changes in seawater δ 7 Li records through Earth's history. Published: 2026-03-20T00:44:17+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.539 (consider)

Authors: Longfei Xu; Yishan Cheng; Jinchuan Zhang; Yang Liu; Xingxu Zhao; Yuhang Sun

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123378

Relevance: 0.539 (consider)

Variations in seawater lithium isotopes (δ 7 Li) are commonly linked to changes in continental silicate weathering fluxes. However, the influence of geothermal fluid input on riverine lithium, particularly in tectonically active regions, remains poorly constrained. Here, samples from hot springs along the Lancang River (LCR) in the southeastern Tibetan Plateau (TP) were collected for analysis of hydrochemistry, Li isotope composition (δ 7 Li) and water-rock interactions to study the geochemical characteristics of Li in geothermal waters and corresponding impact on riverine Li. Geothermal waters exhibit high Li concentrations (Li geo , 0.04–2.1 mg/L) and low Li isotope ratios (δ 7 Li geo , −0.56 to +12.3‰). High reservoir temperatures along the LCR (93–176 °C) facilitate water-rock interaction and leach more Li from surrounding rocks into geothermal waters. The Li geo and δ 7 Li geo are influenced by fluid residence time and the dissolution-precipitation balance. Geothermal waters with longer residence time have higher Li geo concentrations, whereas secondary mineral formation preferentially sequesters light Li, resulting in the enrichment of isotopically heavy Li in the residual geothermal fluid. The LCR exhibits elevated mean Li concentration (Li diss ) and depressed mean Li isotope ratio (δ 7 Li diss ) relative to global river averages, with both parameters tracking downstream variations in geothermal inputs. The mixing model estimates that the contribution of geothermal waters decreases downstream. The concomitant downstream increase in δ 7 Li diss may reflect either declining geothermal contribution or increased secondary mineral formation. Our study provides evidence for the significant impact of geothermal water on riverine Li, and therefore potentially linked to changes in seawater δ 7 Li records through Earth's history.
